texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sufficient stochastic maximum principle in a regime-switching diffusion model
texts.text: Sufficient stochastic maximum principle in a regime-switching diffusion model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Catherine Donnelly ∗ Heriot-Watt University, Edinburgh, U.K.
texts.text: Catherine Donnelly ∗ Heriot-Watt University, Edinburgh, U.K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: November 9, 2018
texts.text: November 9, 2018

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We prove a sufficient stochastic maximum principle for the optimal control of a regimeswitching diffusion model. We show the connection to dynamic programming and we apply the result to a quadratic loss minimization problem, which can be used to solve a mean-variance portfolio selection problem.
texts.text: We prove a sufficient stochastic maximum principle for the optimal control of a regimeswitching diffusion model. We show the connection to dynamic programming and we apply the result to a quadratic loss minimization problem, which can be used to solve a mean-variance portfolio selection problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Sufficient maximum principle, regime-switching, optimal control, mean-variance portfolio selection.
texts.text: Keywords: Sufficient maximum principle, regime-switching, optimal control, mean-variance portfolio selection.

texts.label: text

texts.text: Subject classification:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this paper is to prove a sufficient stochastic maximum principle for optimal control within a regime-switching diffusion model. This extends the result of Framstad et al. [2004], which is in a jump-diffusion setting. To prove this, we follow the method in Framstad et al. [2004]. As in their paper, we show the connection to dynamic programming and show how to apply the result to a quadratic loss minimization problem.
texts.text: The aim of this paper is to prove a sufficient stochastic maximum principle for optimal control within a regime-switching diffusion model. This extends the result of Framstad et al. [2004], which is in a jump-diffusion setting. To prove this, we follow the method in Framstad et al. [2004]. As in their paper, we show the connection to dynamic programming and show how to apply the result to a quadratic loss minimization problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An early maximum principle for a diffusion model is in Bismut [1973], where a necessary maximum principle is derived in a model which is somewhat structurally similar to our own and, as we also find in our set-up, this results in jumps in the adjoint variables of the Hamiltonian.
texts.text: An early maximum principle for a diffusion model is in Bismut [1973], where a necessary maximum principle is derived in a model which is somewhat structurally similar to our own and, as we also find in our set-up, this results in jumps in the adjoint variables of the Hamiltonian.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a hidden Markovian regime-switching diffusion model, Elliott et al. [2010] apply, though do not state explicitly, a sufficient maximum principle to a mean-variance portfolio selection problem. However, their model is not the same as the one we consider and hence they do not obtain jumps in the adjoint variables.
texts.text: For a hidden Markovian regime-switching diffusion model, Elliott et al. [2010] apply, though do not state explicitly, a sufficient maximum principle to a mean-variance portfolio selection problem. However, their model is not the same as the one we consider and hence they do not obtain jumps in the adjoint variables.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 2 we detail the regime-switching diffusion model and in Section 3 we set out the control problem. The sufficient stochastic maximum principle is given in Section 4. This is followed by demonstrating in Section 5 the connection with dynamic programming. Finally, in Section 6 we illustrate the use of the sufficient stochastic maximum principle by solving a quadratic loss minimization problem.
texts.text: In Section 2 we detail the regime-switching diffusion model and in Section 3 we set out the control problem. The sufficient stochastic maximum principle is given in Section 4. This is followed by demonstrating in Section 5 the connection with dynamic programming. Finally, in Section 6 we illustrate the use of the sufficient stochastic maximum principle by solving a quadratic loss minimization problem.

texts.orig: 2 The regime-switching diffusion model
texts.text: 2 The regime-switching diffusion model

texts.orig: Let T ∈ (0 , ∞ ) be a fixed, deterministic time. We assume that we are given an N -dimensional Brownian motion W = ( W 1 , . . . , W N ) and a continuous-time, finite state space Markov chain α defined on the same probability space (Ω , F , P ).
texts.text: Let T ∈ (0 , ∞ ) be a fixed, deterministic time. We assume that we are given an N -dimensional Brownian motion W = ( W 1 , . . . , W N ) and a continuous-time, finite state space Markov chain α defined on the same probability space (Ω , F , P ).

[2004, pages 83-84]. This gives J (ˆ u ) -J ( u ) ≥ 0 and hence ˆ u is optimal.
texts.text: We can show that the integrand on the right-hand side of (4.6) is non-negative a.s. for each t ∈ [0 , T ] by fixing the state of the Markov chain and then using the assumed concavity of ˆ H ( x ) to apply the argument of Framstad et al. [2004, pages 83-84]. This gives J (ˆ u ) -J ( u ) ≥ 0 and hence ˆ u is optimal.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Connection to Dynamic Programming
texts.text: 5 Connection to Dynamic Programming

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a jump-diffusion setting, the connection between the stochastic maximum principle and dynamic programming principle is shown in Framstad et al. [2004, Section 3]. We show a similar connection in Theorem 5.1, between the value function V ( t, x, i ) of the control problem and the adjoint processes p ( t ), q ( t ) and η ( t ). The main difference is that, in the regime-switching diffusion model, the adjoint process η ij ( t ) represents the jumps of the x -gradient of the value function due to the Markov chain switching from state i to state j . In the non-regime-switching jump-diffusion model, this adjoint process represents the jumps of the x -gradient of the value function due to the jumps in the state process X ( t ).
texts.text: In a jump-diffusion setting, the connection between the stochastic maximum principle and dynamic programming principle is shown in Framstad et al. [2004, Section 3]. We show a similar connection in Theorem 5.1, between the value function V ( t, x, i ) of the control problem and the adjoint processes p ( t ), q ( t ) and η ( t ). The main difference is that, in the regime-switching diffusion model, the adjoint process η ij ( t ) represents the jumps of the x -gradient of the value function due to the Markov chain switching from state i to state j . In the non-regime-switching jump-diffusion model, this adjoint process represents the jumps of the x -gradient of the value function due to the jumps in the state process X ( t ).

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: STOCHASTIC MAXIMUM PRINCIPLE
texts.text: STOCHASTIC MAXIMUM PRINCIPLE

texts.orig: 6 Application: quadratic loss minimization problem
texts.text: 6 Application: quadratic loss minimization problem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We demonstrate the use of the maximum principle by solving a quadratic loss minimization problem. Consider a regime-switching financial market that is built upon one traded asset, which we call the risky asset, and a risk-free asset. The risk-free asset's price process S 0 = { S 0 ( t ) , t ∈ [0 , T ] } is given by

texts.orig: With this choice of control process and the boundedness conditions on the market parameters r , b and σ , the conditions of Theorem 4.1 are satisfied and hence ˆ u ( t ) is the optimal control process. Remark 6.1 . The above result can be used to obtain the solution to the classical problem of meanvariance portfolio optimization. Suppose we wish to find an admissible portfolio process which minimizes var( X ( T )) = E ( X ( T ) -E( X ( T ))) 2 subject to E( X ( T )) = a , for some a ∈ R . Applying a Lagrange multiplier technique, we note that for all λ ∈ R ,
texts.text: With this choice of control process and the boundedness conditions on the market parameters r , b and σ , the conditions of Theorem 4.1 are satisfied and hence ˆ u ( t ) is the optimal control process. Remark 6.1 . The above result can be used to obtain the solution to the classical problem of meanvariance portfolio optimization. Suppose we wish to find an admissible portfolio process which minimizes var( X ( T )) = E ( X ( T ) -E( X ( T ))) 2 subject to E( X ( T )) = a , for some a ∈ R . Applying a Lagrange multiplier technique, we note that for all λ ∈ R ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fix λ ∈ R and minimize E ( X ( T ) -a + λ ) 2 . The portfolio process which minimizes this is ˆ u ( t ) := ˆ u ( t ; λ ), which is given by (6.24) with d := a -λ . Then we maximize the quadratic function E( X ( T ) -a + λ ) 2 -λ 2 over all λ ∈ R to find the optimal λ ⋆ ∈ R and hence we obtain the optimal portfolio process ˆ u ( t ; λ ⋆ ) which solves the mean-variance problem.
texts.text: Fix λ ∈ R and minimize E ( X ( T ) -a + λ ) 2 . The portfolio process which minimizes this is ˆ u ( t ) := ˆ u ( t ; λ ), which is given by (6.24) with d := a -λ . Then we maximize the quadratic function E( X ( T ) -a + λ ) 2 -λ 2 over all λ ∈ R to find the optimal λ ⋆ ∈ R and hence we obtain the optimal portfolio process ˆ u ( t ; λ ⋆ ) which solves the mean-variance problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 6.2 . The optimal control process for the mean-variance problem was also found in Zhou and Yin [2003] using a stochastic LQ control technique and completion-of-squares.
texts.text: Remark 6.2 . The optimal control process for the mean-variance problem was also found in Zhou and Yin [2003] using a stochastic LQ control technique and completion-of-squares.
